svg.text: Tracing dijkstra from source A on the 5-node example graph: A–B(4), A–C(2), B–C(3), B–D(5), C–E(1), D–E(3)
the starting vertex — we find shortest paths FROM this
the vertex just popped from the min-heap (now finalized)
a neighbor of u we're checking for a shorter path
number of vertices in the graph (used in Big O)
best known distance from source to vertex x
the cost of the edge between u and v
priority queue that always returns the smallest
number of edges in the graph (used in Big O)
How does the trace know which vertex to pop next?
The min-heap always returns its smallest element — min_heap.top() gives it, min_heap.pop() removes it. There's no separate "find minimum" step.
Example: after step 1 pushes {4, B} and {2, C}, step 2 pops {2, C} — 2 is the smallest key sitting at the top of the heap.
no improvements — v=A skip, v=C skip, v=D skip (6 < 4+5=9)
no improvements — v=B skip, v=E skip
Done — heap empty. Shortest distances from A: B=4, C=2, D=6, E=3
No stale pops occurred here — each vertex’s first pushed distance was already optimal. When a later relax improves dist[v], the old {old_dist, v} entry becomes stale and is skipped on pop.
How the Shortest Path Tree Grows
Green filled = finalized Blue outline = in heap Gray = unvisited Solid line = tree edge Dashed = tentative
→ Continue to dijkstra_result.svg for the shortest path tree visualization and key facts
